candidate_problems.text: Does the conjecture overstate the role of historical design intent? A key open question is whether incompleteness genuinely depends on the specific formalist shift toward mechanical rule-following and Hilbert-style self-certification, or whether it follows more deeply from semantic expressiveness plus representability of syntax regardless of that historical origin. Clarifying this would test whether the conjecture is a true explanatory advance or mainly a reframing of known results.

Can the systems/feedback interpretation be made mathematically load-bearing rather than metaphorical? The conjecture claims that internal self-description introduces feedback and makes completeness plus consistency an overconstrained design target. A worthwhile problem is to specify exactly which formal features correspond to feedback, what instability means in theorem-proving terms, and whether this systems language yields new distinctions, predictions, or generalizations beyond standard recursion-theoretic and proof-theoretic accounts.

What is the precise scope of the claimed limit on 'self-vindication'? The conjecture says sufficiently strong consistent systems cannot fully internalize a correct account of their own reliability, but there are many nearby notions: proving consistency, proving soundness for classes of sentences, reflection principles, relative consistency, and partial internal metatheory. An important open question is where the boundary actually lies and whether the conjecture can distinguish impossible self-certification from the many substantial forms of internal self-knowledge that formal systems do achieve.
